From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American cinematographer (1935–2020)

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer) "Special:EditPage/Michael Chapman (cinematographer)") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Michael Chapman" cinematographer](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Michael+Chapman%22+cinematographer&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2020)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

| Michael Chapman | |
| --- | --- |
| The Portrait of Michael Chapman 2020 - Chalk on paper 36" x 22" | |
| Born | Michael Crawford Chapman  (1935-11-21)November 21, 1935  New York City, New York, U.S. |
| Died | September 20, 2020(2020-09-20) (aged 84)  Los Angeles, California, U.S. |
| Occupations | * Cinematographer * film director |
| Years active | 1968–2007 |
| Spouse | [Amy Holden Jones](/wiki/Amy_Holden_Jones "Amy Holden Jones") |

**Michael Crawford Chapman**, [ASC](/wiki/American_Society_of_Cinematographers "American Society of Cinematographers") (November 21, 1935 – September 20, 2020) was an American [cinematographer](/wiki/Cinematographer "Cinematographer") and [film director](/wiki/Film_director "Film director"), known for his work on many films of the [American New Wave](/wiki/New_Hollywood "New Hollywood") during the 1970s and the 1980s. He was twice nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Cinematography](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Cinematography "Academy Award for Best Cinematography"), for [*Raging Bull*](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull") (1980) and [*The Fugitive*](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1993_film) "The Fugitive (1993 film)") (1993). He received the [American Society of Cinematographers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Cinematographers "American Society of Cinematographers")' Lifetime Achievement Award in 2004.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Chapman was born in New York City in 1935, but raised in [Wellesley, Massachusetts](/wiki/Wellesley,_Massachusetts "Wellesley, Massachusetts"), a suburb of Boston, without much of an interest in film. As a youth, he was more interested in sports than photography or painting. He graduated from high school at [Andover](/wiki/Phillips_Academy "Phillips Academy"), a college preparatory school in Andover, Massachusetts.[[1]](#cite_note-interview-1) After high school, he attended [Columbia College](/wiki/Columbia_College_(New_York) "Columbia College (New York)"), where he majored in English. Upon his graduation, he worked temporarily as a [brakeman](/wiki/Brakeman "Brakeman") for the [Erie Lackawanna Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Lackawanna_Railway "Erie Lackawanna Railway") in the Midwest. In 1958, he was drafted into the [U.S. Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"), where he served in the [Signal Corps](/wiki/United_States_Army_Signal_Corps "United States Army Signal Corps") stationed in New Jersey and [Thule Air Base](/wiki/Thule_Air_Base "Thule Air Base") in Greenland.[[1]](#cite_note-interview-1)

Chapman's father-in-law, [Joe Brun](/wiki/Joseph_C._Brun "Joseph C. Brun"), got him his first job in the industry: working as an assistant cameraman and focus puller on commercials, as there were not enough feature films being shot in New York at the time.

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

Chapman began his film career as a camera operator, distinguishing himself on [Francis Ford Coppola](/wiki/Francis_Ford_Coppola "Francis Ford Coppola")'s *[The Godfather](/wiki/The_Godfather "The Godfather")* (1972) and [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg")'s *[Jaws](/wiki/Jaws_(film) "Jaws (film)")* (1975) before making the leap to cinematographer. He fondly remembered his time as an operator and called it one of the best jobs in the movie business because "you get to see the film before anyone else does!"

As a cinematographer, he became known for his two collaborations with [Martin Scorsese](/wiki/Martin_Scorsese "Martin Scorsese"): *[Taxi Driver](/wiki/Taxi_Driver "Taxi Driver")* (1976) and *[Raging Bull](/wiki/Raging_Bull "Raging Bull")* (1980). Chapman was also the cinematographer for the remake of *[Invasion of the Body Snatchers](/wiki/Invasion_of_the_Body_Snatchers_(1978_film) "Invasion of the Body Snatchers (1978 film)")* (1978). He and Scorsese were huge fans of [The Band](/wiki/The_Band "The Band"), and Chapman served as the principal cinematographer for their documentary on The Band, called *[The Last Waltz](/wiki/The_Last_Waltz "The Last Waltz")* (1978). With nine cameras shooting at once, Chapman remembered that "the strategy for filming all of their songs was planned out in enormous detail."

Chapman's style tended to feature high contrasts and an aggressive use of strong colors. He was also adept at setting up complex camera movements quickly and improvising on the set. This style was epitomized in the boxing sequences in *Raging Bull*, during which the camera was often strapped to an actor through improvised rigs. His bold use of black-and-white cinematography on *Raging Bull* proved particularly difficult and earned Chapman his first Academy Award nomination. As with his work on *Jaws,* Chapman used a handheld camera to shoot much of the film.

Besides his work with Scorsese, Chapman worked as Director of Photography for directors [Hal Ashby](/wiki/Hal_Ashby "Hal Ashby"), [Philip Kaufman](/wiki/Philip_Kaufman "Philip Kaufman"), [Martin Ritt](/wiki/Martin_Ritt "Martin Ritt"), [Robert Towne](/wiki/Robert_Towne "Robert Towne"), [Michael Caton-Jones](/wiki/Michael_Caton-Jones "Michael Caton-Jones"), [Andrew Davis](/wiki/Andrew_Davis_(director) "Andrew Davis (director)"), and [Ivan Reitman](/wiki/Ivan_Reitman "Ivan Reitman"). He occasionally made small cameos in films that he shot; he had also directed several films of his own, the best known being *[All the Right Moves](/wiki/All_the_Right_Moves_(film) "All the Right Moves (film)")* (1983), starring [Tom Cruise](/wiki/Tom_Cruise "Tom Cruise") in one of his earliest roles.

In 1987, Chapman collaborated again with Scorsese on the 18-minute short film that served as the music video for [Michael Jackson](/wiki/Michael_Jackson "Michael Jackson")'s *[Bad](/wiki/Bad_(Michael_Jackson_song) "Bad (Michael Jackson song)")*.

Chapman also shot a string of comedies in the late 1980s and early 1990s, such as *[Ghostbusters II](/wiki/Ghostbusters_II "Ghostbusters II")* and *[Kindergarten Cop](/wiki/Kindergarten_Cop "Kindergarten Cop"),* and admitted that he did not need to alter his style very much. But he has said, "On comedies, I use a little more fill light; you tend to create a lit atmosphere where the performers can be at home, where they can move around…without having to hit a precise mark." He became a member of the [American Society of Cinematographers](/wiki/American_Society_of_Cinematographers "American Society of Cinematographers") (ASC) in 1995.[[2]](#cite_note-variety2020-2)

His final film was *[Bridge to Terabithia](/wiki/Bridge_to_Terabithia_(2007_film) "Bridge to Terabithia (2007 film)")* (2007). According to the DVD commentary, Chapman planned to retire after the film was finished, saying he would like to have the last film he shot be a good one.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Chapman was married to screenwriter [Amy Holden Jones](/wiki/Amy_Holden_Jones "Amy Holden Jones"). His father-in-law, [Joe Brun](/wiki/Joseph_C._Brun "Joseph C. Brun"), was an Oscar-nominated cinematographer who had emigrated from France in the early 20th century.

He stated later in his life that he no longer watched films directed by frequent collaborators Martin Scorsese or [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg"), as he knew their general style would not change much. "Unless a director makes some huge sea change in what he does, that the work, the mechanical work, is going to be vaguely the same — or of the same school, anyway — but what changes is the intelligence and passion behind it in the script." He also admitted that his preferred method was to watch movies at home and that he rarely, if ever, went to a theater any more.

[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_Chapman_(cinematographer)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death")]

Chapman died from [congestive heart failure](/wiki/Congestive_heart_failure "Congestive heart failure") on September 20, 2020, at his home in Los Angeles.[[4]](#cite_note-thr2020-4)
